perturbation.original_text: At all times, Administrator shall comply with all laws, rules and regulations applicable to a transfer agent under the Federal securities laws, including without limitation, requirements for delivery of prospectuses (which term includes prospectus supplements). Whether or not required by applicable law, Administrator shall deliver or arrange for the delivery of prospectuses to Plan Representatives and to Participants in Participant-directed Plans.
perturbation.changed_text: At all times, Administrator shall comply with all laws, rules and regulations applicable to a transfer agent under the Federal securities laws. Administrator may deliver prospectuses to Plan Representatives at its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the mandatory delivery of prospectuses to Participants in Participant-directed Plans introduces a legal risk because it contradicts the SEC's regulations regarding the provision of information to investors.
perturbation.contradicted_law: SEC Rule 498 under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended (17 CFR § 230.498)
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 498 mandates that a summary prospectus be provided to investors. By making prospectus delivery discretionary rather than mandatory, the modified text violates the requirement that investors receive key information about the fund. The original text acknowledges delivery is necessary 'whether or not required by applicable law', this is then replaced to 'Administrator may deliver prospectuses', removing a consumer's ability to make informed investment choices.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.498 - Summary Prospectuses for open-end management investment companies. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.498 - Summary Prospectuses for open-end management investment companies. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.498 Summary Prospectuses for open-end management investment companies. (a) Definitions. For purposes of this section: (1) Class means a class of shares issued by a Fund that has more than one class that represent interests in the same portfolio of securities under § 270.18f-3 of this chapter or under an order exempting the Fund from sections 18(f), 18(g), and 18(i) of the Investment Company Act ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-18(f) , 80a-18(g) , and 80a-18(i) ). (2) Exchange-Traded Fund means a Fund or a Class , the shares of which are traded on a national securities exchange, and that has formed and operates pursuant to an exemptive order granted by the Commission or in reliance on an exemptive rule adopted by the Commission . (3) Fund means an open-end management investment company, or any Series of such a company, that has, or is included in, an effective registration statement on Form N-1A ( §§ 239.15A and 274.11A of this chapter) and that has a current prospectus that satisfies the requirements of section 10(a) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 77j(a) ). (4) Series means shares offered by a Fund that represent undivided interests in a portfolio of investments and that are preferred over all other series of shares for assets specifically allocated to that series in accordance with § 270.18f-2(a) of this chapter. (5) Statement of Additional Information means the statement of additional information required by Part B of Form N-1A. (6) Statutory Prospectus means a prospectus that satisfies the requirements of section 10(a) of the Act . (7) Summary Prospectus means the summary prospectus described in paragraph (b) of this section. (b) General requirements
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the mandatory delivery of prospectuses to Participants in Participant-directed Plans. The Law Explanation states that 'SEC Rule 498 mandates that a summary prospectus be provided to investors.' Scraped Law Snippet 2 references '17 CFR § 230.498 - Summary Prospectuses for open-end management investment companies.' By changing 'Administrator shall deliver' to 'Administrator may deliver,' the revised text allows for non-delivery of prospectuses, directly contradicting the requirement of providing a summary prospectus to investors, thus creating an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Administrator will adopt and maintain an anti-money laundering program in compliance with applicable laws and regulations, including provisions for necessary currency transaction reporting, detection of suspicious activities that could give rise to money laundering, and knowing Administrator's customers.
perturbation.changed_text: Administrator will adopt and maintain an anti-money laundering program.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific provisions for currency transaction reporting, detection of suspicious activities, and knowing the customer weakens the anti-money laundering program and makes it non-compliant with federal regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 31 CFR § 1020.210 mandates that financial institutions establish a Customer Identification Program (CIP) that includes specific procedures for identifying customers and verifying their identities. The modified text, by omitting the provisions for currency transaction reporting, detecting suspicious activity, and KYC, contradicts the requirements of the CIP rule. The original text acknowledges the components for AML, KYC, and suspicious activity, but it is then removed, resulting in a weakened consumer protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified text omits key components of an anti-money laundering program, specifically currency transaction reporting, detection of suspicious activities, and knowing the customer. The Law Explanation states that 31 CFR § 1020.210 mandates specific procedures for identifying customers and verifying their identities which the modified text now contradicts by omission, creating a legal contradiction, as per the provided definition of Omission Legal Contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 31 CFR 1020.210, corroborating the Law Explanation's reference to the regulation. This regulation outlines the requirements for AML programs which the modified text weakens by removing essential elements, leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Administrator agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the Trust, the Trust's administrators, investment adviser, and transfer agent, and each of their directors, trustees, officers, employees, agents and each person, if any, who controls them within the meaning of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the "Securities Act"), against any losses, claims, damages, liabilities or expenses to which an indemnitee may become subject insofar as those losses, claims, damages, liabilities or expenses (or actions in respect thereof) arise out of or are based upon (i) any orders that are not timely transmitted by Administrator in accordance with Section 4 of this Agreement or any trades that are cancelled 6 by the Trust or its designee based upon payments for purchases of Shares that are not timely wired; (ii) Administrator's negligence or willful misconduct in performing the Services; (iii) any breach by Administrator of any material representation, warranty or covenant made in this Agreement; or (iv) any requests that are submitted by duly authorized representatives of Administrator on behalf of Participants or Plan Representatives for transaction adjustments (including, but not limited to, the pricing of net purchases or net redemptions of Shares on an "as of" basis). Administrator will reimburse the indemnities for any legal or other expenses reasonably incurred, as incurred, by them in connection with investigating or defending such losses, claims or actions.
perturbation.changed_text: Administrator agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the Trust against any losses, claims, damages, liabilities or expenses to which an indemnitee may become subject insofar as those losses, claims, damages, liabilities or expenses (or actions in respect thereof) arise out of or are based upon (ii) Administrator's negligence or willful misconduct in performing the Services; (iii) any breach by Administrator of any material representation, warranty or covenant made in this Agreement. Administrator will reimburse the indemnities for any legal expenses incurred in connection with investigating or defending such losses, claims or actions.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to the Securities Act of 1933 and limiting the indemnification to only the Trust significantly narrows the scope of protection for related parties. This creates a legal contradiction because it potentially weakens the liability coverage required under securities regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11 of the Securities Act of 1933 imposes liability on various parties involved in the registration statement if it contains false or misleading information. By narrowing the indemnification to exclude directors, trustees, officers, employees, agents, and controlling persons, the modified text weakens the ability of these individuals to seek recourse for liabilities arising from misleading registration statements, potentially contradicting the intent of the Securities Act to protect investors. The original clause specifically states the intent to indemnify those within the definition of the Securities Act, while the changed text does not.

Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement (a) Persons possessing cause of action; persons liable In case any part of the registration statement, when such part became effective, contained an untrue statement of a material fact or omitted to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the state
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the indemnification obligations, particularly by removing coverage for the Trust's administrators, investment adviser, transfer agent, and their associated personnel as defined under the Securities Act of 1933. The original text specifically indemnified these parties against losses arising from various actions, including those related to the Securities Act. The changed text, by limiting indemnification to only the Trust, contradicts the intent of the Securities Act to protect investors by ensuring that individuals responsible for registration statements can be held liable for misstatements or omissions. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 15 USC 77k, which discusses civil liabilities on account of false registration statements. By narrowing the indemnification scope, the modified contract potentially hinders the ability of those individuals to seek recourse for liabilities arising from misleading registration statements, which runs counter to the Securities Act.
